description:
Capacity-planning scenario – “Lightweight on-call log backup”

 Context:
 • Your working directory is /home/user.
 • A folder already exists at /home/user/capacity/logs/ that currently holds three
   plain-text application log files.

   /home/user/capacity/logs/app.log  
   /home/user/capacity/logs/db.log  
   /home/user/capacity/logs/sys.log  

   (Their exact contents do not matter for your shell workflow; they are just small
   text files.)

 Goal:
 1. Create a new directory called /home/user/capacity/backup if it does not already
    exist.  (Do NOT touch any other paths.)
 2. Produce a single compressed archive named  
      /home/user/capacity/backup/logs_backup.tar.gz  
    that contains the three *.log files from /home/user/capacity/logs.  
    • The archive must NOT include any parent directory structure; when the archive
      is extracted with `tar -xzf`, it should place the three log files directly in
      the current working directory.
 3. Generate an ASCII report file  
      /home/user/capacity/backup/capacity_report.txt  
    following exactly the format described below.

 Required format for capacity_report.txt
 • Each line must contain two tokens separated by exactly one ASCII space:
      <filename> <size_in_bytes>
 • The first three lines must be the individual log files listed in strict
   case-sensitive alphabetical order (app.log, db.log, sys.log).
 • The fourth and final line must begin with the word TOTAL, followed by a single
   space, followed by the sum of the sizes from the three preceding lines.
 • No extra spaces, no tab characters, and **no trailing whitespace** at the ends
   of lines.
 • The file must contain a terminating newline character (i.e., “\n”) after the
   final TOTAL line.

 Example of the exact required layout (values will differ in other scenarios):

   app.log 123
   db.log 456
   sys.log 789
   TOTAL 1368

 Constraints:
 • Accomplish the whole task with a straightforward sequence of normal shell
   commands (roughly three to four commands are enough, although you may use more
   if you prefer).
 • You do NOT need super-user privileges at any point.
 • Do not delete or rename the original log files.

 Verification notes for the automated grader:
 • /home/user/capacity/backup/ must exist and be a directory.
 • /home/user/capacity/backup/logs_backup.tar.gz must exist, be a regular file,
   and its compressed payload must contain exactly three entries: app.log, db.log,
   sys.log — without any leading directory paths inside the archive.
 • /home/user/capacity/backup/capacity_report.txt must exist and match the format
   rules above byte-for-byte, including the final newline.
 • The size values reported inside capacity_report.txt must correspond to the
   actual byte sizes (as returned by `stat -c%s <file>`) of the three original
   log files located in /home/user/capacity/logs/.

 Your shell is ready. Carry out the backup and produce the report.
truth:
Initial filesystem setup before the agent starts
 ────────────────────────────────────────────────
 1. Directory /home/user/capacity/logs/ already exists.
 2. The three log files and their *exact* byte lengths are:

    • /home/user/capacity/logs/app.log  
      Content (15 bytes):  
        APP_LOG_SAMPLE\n

    • /home/user/capacity/logs/db.log  
      Content (14 bytes):  
        DB_LOG_SAMPLE\n

    • /home/user/capacity/logs/sys.log  
      Content (15 bytes):  
        SYS_LOG_SAMPLE\n

    (Note: “\n” represents a single ASCII newline character.)

    Calculated byte sizes: app.log = 15, db.log = 14, sys.log = 15

    Total = 44

 Expected artifacts after successful agent run
 ──────────────────────────────────────────────
 1. Directory /home/user/capacity/backup/ exists (0700 or default user umask).
 2. Archive file /home/user/capacity/backup/logs_backup.tar.gz exists.
    • Running:  
        tar -tzf /home/user/capacity/backup/logs_backup.tar.gz
      must output exactly:
        app.log
        db.log
        sys.log
      (in that order or any order; order is not graded, only entry names.)
 3. File /home/user/capacity/backup/capacity_report.txt exists and contains the
    following exact 4 lines (including a final newline):

      app.log 15
      db.log 14
      sys.log 15
      TOTAL 44

    Byte-for-byte identical, with a newline after “44”.
